Brows & Lashes
June 16, 2026 · 4 min read

Brow Lamination vs Microblading — Which One Is Right for You?

Both give you fuller, defined brows — but they work very differently and suit different lifestyles. We break it down so you can walk in knowing exactly what you want.

If you've been scrolling Instagram lately, you've probably seen both brow lamination and microblading everywhere. And if you're wondering which one is right for you, you're not alone — it's one of the questions we get asked most at Mammie's Beauty Palace.

Brow Lamination is a chemical treatment that relaxes and sets your natural brow hairs in a sleek upward direction — giving you that fluffy, groomed, editorial look. It's non-invasive, lasts 6–8 weeks, and is perfect if you have enough brow hair but it grows in all directions. At Mammie's, we pair it with a tint for maximum definition. From R300.

Microblading is a semi-permanent technique that uses a fine blade to deposit pigment into the skin, mimicking individual brow hairs. It's ideal if you have sparse or patchy brows and lasts 12–18 months. It does require a healing period and occasional touch-ups.

Our honest take: If your brows are full but unruly — go lamination. If you wake up with barely-there brows — microblading changes your life. Not sure? Book a brow consultation with Kgomotso, our brow specialist, and she'll assess your natural hair density and recommend the best route.

"Great brows frame your entire face — getting them right is the single biggest beauty upgrade most people never try." — Kgomotso, Brow & Lash Specialist

Read Full Article → Book Brow Service
Skin Care
June 12, 2026 · 5 min read

Why Your Skin Is Breaking Out — And What a Monthly Facial Actually Does

Acne doesn't just happen to teenagers. If your skin has been acting up, here's the real reason why — and how a professional facial is different from anything you do at home.

Adult acne is one of the most frustrating skin concerns we see at Mammie's — and it's almost always misunderstood. Most people reach for harsh products that strip the skin, which makes the problem worse. Here's what's actually happening.

The real causes of adult breakouts: Hormonal fluctuations, stress (which spikes cortisol and increases oil production), sleeping on unwashed pillowcases, touching your face throughout the day, and — surprisingly — over-cleansing. When your skin gets too dry, it compensates by producing more oil.

What a professional facial does that home care can't: Our Acne Treatment Facial (R450) uses professional-grade products to deeply cleanse, extract blocked pores safely (doing this yourself at home causes scarring), apply active ingredients at the right concentration, and leave the skin barrier intact. The Deep Clean Facial (R400) is great as a maintenance treatment once a month to stop breakouts before they start.

The Mammie's skin routine we recommend:

  • Monthly Acne Treatment or Deep Clean Facial
  • Gentle, non-stripping cleanser at home (morning and night)
  • SPF every single morning — even on cloudy days
  • Change your pillowcase every 3–4 days

"Consistency beats intensity every time when it comes to skin. One monthly facial is worth more than ten different products used randomly." — Buhle, Facial & Skin Specialist

Read Full Article → Book a Facial
Nail Care
June 8, 2026 · 3 min read

5 Reasons Your Gel Nails Keep Lifting — And How to Make Them Last 3 Weeks+

Gel nails should last at least 2–3 weeks. If yours are peeling after a few days, one of these five things is almost certainly the reason — and every one of them is fixable.

Nothing is more frustrating than freshly done nails that start lifting within days. We hear this a lot at Mammie's — and it almost always comes down to one of these five things.

1. Your nails weren't prepped properly. This is the number one cause. Any oil, moisture or residue left on the nail plate stops the gel from bonding. At Mammie's, our nail artists dehydrate and prime each nail before any product touches it — this step alone makes the difference between 1 week and 3 weeks.

2. The gel was applied too thick. Thick layers don't cure properly all the way through, which means the bottom layer stays soft and lifts. Thin, even layers cure fully and grip better.

3. You have naturally oily nail beds. Some people just produce more natural oils than others — it doesn't mean gel won't work, it means the prep step matters even more. Tell your nail artist and they'll adjust the routine.

4. The edges weren't capped. The free edge of your nail is where lifting always starts. Sealing the tip of each nail with a thin stripe of gel is a small step that adds days to your wear.

5. Exposure to water too soon. For the first 24 hours after your appointment, try to keep your hands dry. Doing dishes, washing hair or long baths in the first day dramatically increases the chance of early lifting.

Pro tip for longevity: Apply a thin layer of cuticle oil every day. It keeps the skin around your nails healthy and flexible, which reduces the stress that causes chips.

"Our Gel Nails (R350) and Acrylic Nails (R450) are done with professional-grade products and proper prep — if yours aren't lasting, come in and let us show you the difference." — Lerato, Nail Artist

Ntombikayise — Founder The heart behind Mammie's
Our Story

Where every client feels seen

Mammie's Beauty Palace began with a simple belief: that beauty care should feel like being looked after, not processed. What started as one woman's gift for making people feel radiant has grown into a full beauty palace in Ironside — hair, nails, skin, brows, lashes and makeup, all under one roof.

The name "Mammie" carries that same warmth — the comfort of being cared for by someone who knows exactly what you need, even before you say it. Every specialist here trained with that philosophy: skilled hands, a calm space, and an unhurried appointment that leaves you not just looking, but feeling, different.

Today, Mammie's Beauty Palace exists to create a beauty sanctuary where every client feels confident, radiant, beautiful and cared for — supported by a digital concierge that remembers your preferences, so the personal touch never gets lost as we grow.

— BLOG1 —
Blog / Brows & Lashes

Brow Lamination vs Microblading — Which One Is Right for You?

June 16, 2026  ·  4 min read  ·  By Kgomotso, Brow & Lash Specialist

If you've been scrolling Instagram lately, you've probably seen both brow lamination and microblading everywhere. Both promise fuller, more defined brows — but they're completely different treatments that work in completely different ways. And choosing the wrong one for your hair type and lifestyle could mean months of disappointment.

At Mammie's Beauty Palace, brow consultations are one of the most common conversations we have. So here's everything you need to know before you book.

What Is Brow Lamination?

Brow lamination is a non-invasive chemical treatment that relaxes and sets your natural brow hairs in a sleek, upward direction — giving you that fluffy, groomed, editorial finish you see on every beauty influencer right now.

The process involves applying a lifting solution to the brow hairs (not the skin), combing them into the desired direction, then setting them with a neutralising solution. The result lasts 6–8 weeks and is completely reversible — your brows grow back exactly as before.

At Mammie's, we pair brow lamination with a tint to deepen and define the colour at the same time. The combination gives you polished, full brows with zero daily effort. From R300.

Brow lamination is ideal if:

  • You have enough natural brow hair but it grows in different directions
  • You want a low-maintenance, natural-looking result
  • You're not ready to commit to a semi-permanent treatment
  • You want to try out a fuller brow look before going permanent

What Is Microblading?

Microblading is a semi-permanent cosmetic tattooing technique that uses a fine hand-held tool with tiny needles to deposit pigment into the upper layers of the skin. Each stroke mimics an individual brow hair, creating hyper-realistic results that fill in sparse, patchy or over-plucked brows.

Unlike brow lamination which works with your existing hair, microblading adds the appearance of hair where there is little or none. Results typically last 12–18 months, after which the pigment fades and a touch-up is recommended.

Microblading is ideal if:

  • You have sparse, thin or patchy brows with limited natural hair
  • You want to wake up with defined brows without any daily pencilling
  • You're comfortable with a short healing period (7–14 days of mild flaking)
  • You want long-lasting results and are happy to top up annually

Side-by-Side Comparison

Brow Lamination
  • Non-invasive — no needles, no pigment
  • Works with your natural hair
  • Results last 6–8 weeks
  • No downtime or healing period
  • Fully reversible
  • From R300
Microblading
  • Semi-permanent cosmetic tattoo
  • Adds pigment into the skin
  • Results last 12–18 months
  • Short healing period required
  • Touch-up needed after 6–12 months
  • Priced on consultation

Which One Should You Choose?

Here's our simple rule of thumb at Mammie's:

If your brows are full but unruly — go with brow lamination. It will tame, define and elevate what you already have.

If you wake up with barely-there brows — microblading will genuinely change your daily routine and your confidence.

If you're not sure — come in for a brow consultation first. Kgomotso, our dedicated brow and lash specialist, will assess your natural hair density, skin type and desired look, and give you an honest recommendation before any treatment is booked.

"Great brows frame your entire face — getting them right is the single biggest beauty upgrade most people never try. And the best part? You don't need to overdo it. A well-shaped, well-defined brow does all the heavy lifting."
— Kgomotso, Brow & Lash Specialist, Mammie's Beauty Palace

Aftercare Tips — Brow Lamination

  • Avoid getting your brows wet for the first 24 hours
  • Don't rub or apply heavy products over the brow area for 48 hours
  • Apply a small amount of nourishing oil (castor oil works well) to the brows every few days to keep the hairs conditioned
  • Avoid steam rooms and saunas for the first week
  • Brush your brows upward gently each morning with a spoolie to maintain the direction

Ready to transform your brows?

Book a Brow Lamination + Tint, a Brow Shaping session, or a free consultation with Kgomotso — all available online.

— BLOG2 —
Blog / Skin Care

Why Your Skin Is Breaking Out — And What a Monthly Facial Actually Does

June 12, 2026  ·  5 min read  ·  By Buhle, Facial & Skin Specialist

Acne doesn't just happen to teenagers. Adult breakouts — the kind that appear on your chin, jaw, cheeks and forehead seemingly out of nowhere — are one of the most frustrating and misunderstood skin concerns we see at Mammie's Beauty Palace every single week.

And the most common mistake people make? Reaching for the harshest products they can find to "dry it out." That approach almost always makes things worse. Here's what's actually happening — and what to do instead.

The Real Causes of Adult Breakouts

Most adult acne isn't caused by dirt or not washing your face enough. The real drivers are:

Hormonal fluctuations. Estrogen, progesterone and testosterone all affect how much oil your skin produces. This is why breakouts tend to appear around your cycle, during pregnancy, or when stress levels spike. Jawline and chin acne in particular is almost always hormone-related.

Stress. When you're stressed, your body releases cortisol — and cortisol signals your sebaceous glands to produce more oil. More oil means more blocked pores. More blocked pores mean breakouts. Managing stress isn't just good for your mental health; it's genuinely good for your skin.

Your pillowcase. You spend 7–8 hours with your face pressed against fabric that collects oil, sweat, hair products and bacteria every single night. If you're not changing your pillowcase every 3–4 days, it's contributing to your breakouts.

Over-cleansing and harsh products. This is the counterintuitive one. When you strip your skin's natural oils with strong cleansers or exfoliants, your skin compensates by producing more oil — which leads to more breakouts. The goal is to clean gently, not aggressively.

Touching your face. Your hands pick up bacteria from every surface you touch throughout the day. Every time you touch, rest your chin on your hand, or rub your eyes, you're transferring that to your skin. It adds up.

What a Professional Facial Does That Home Care Simply Can't

Home skincare is important — but it has limits. Here's what a professional facial at Mammie's does that your bathroom cabinet can't replicate:

Deep extraction done safely. Extracting blocked pores at home — squeezing, picking, using pore strips — almost always causes scarring, hyperpigmentation and introduces more bacteria into the skin. Our specialists use professional extraction techniques and tools that clear the pore without damaging the surrounding tissue.

Professional-grade actives at the right concentration. Salicylic acid, glycolic acid, niacinamide and retinoids all work — but only at concentrations and in formulations that are calibrated for your skin type. Consumer products are intentionally diluted for safety. The products we use in our facials are professional-strength, applied by someone who understands how your skin is responding in real time.

Skin barrier preservation. One of the most common outcomes of over-aggressive home routines is a compromised skin barrier — skin that's red, reactive, tight and breaking out because it's been stripped. A skilled facial specialist knows how to deliver active treatment while keeping the barrier intact.

Personalised assessment. Every time you come in, Buhle or one of our skin specialists looks at what your skin is doing right now — not what it was doing six months ago. Treatments are adjusted accordingly.

Mammie's Acne Treatment Facial (R450) is specifically formulated for acne-prone skin — it calms inflammation, safely clears blocked pores, and applies targeted actives without stripping. Paired with a monthly Deep Clean Facial (R400), most clients see consistent improvement within 6–8 weeks.

Our Recommended Routine for Acne-Prone Skin

This is the routine we send clients home with after their first acne facial at Mammie's. It's simple on purpose — consistency matters more than complexity.

  • Morning: Gentle, non-stripping cleanser → lightweight moisturiser → SPF (every day, even indoors)
  • Evening: Double cleanse (oil cleanser first, then water-based) → targeted treatment if prescribed → moisturiser
  • Weekly: Single gentle chemical exfoliant (low-percentage AHA or BHA) — not scrubs
  • Monthly: Acne Treatment Facial or Deep Clean Facial at Mammie's
  • Lifestyle: Change pillowcase every 3–4 days. Stop touching your face. Drink water. Sleep.

Which Facial Is Right for You?

If you're not sure where to start, here's a quick guide to our facial menu:

  • Acne Treatment Facial (R450) — Active acne, inflammation, clogged pores
  • Deep Clean Facial (R400) — General congestion and oily skin maintenance
  • Hydrating Facial (R420) — Dry, dehydrated or sensitive skin
  • Brightening Facial (R480) — Dullness, uneven skin tone, post-acne marks

Still not sure? Book a skin consultation — it's included with your first facial — and let Buhle assess your skin in person and recommend the right starting point.

"Consistency beats intensity every time when it comes to skin. One monthly professional facial is worth more than ten different products used randomly — because it works with your skin's actual state, not a generic assumption about what it needs."
— Buhle, Facial & Skin Specialist, Mammie's Beauty Palace

Start your clear skin journey

Book an Acne Treatment Facial, Deep Clean Facial or a free skin consultation at Mammie's Beauty Palace in Ironside.

5 Reasons Your Gel Nails Keep Lifting — And How to Make Them Last 3 Weeks+

June 8, 2026  ·  3 min read  ·  By Lerato, Nail Artist

Gel nails should last at least 2–3 weeks with normal day-to-day wear. If yours are peeling, chipping or lifting within a few days of your appointment, something went wrong — either in the application, the aftercare, or both. The good news is every single cause is fixable.

As a nail artist at Mammie's Beauty Palace, lifting nails are the most common complaint I hear from clients who come in from other salons. Here's exactly what causes it and how we prevent it.

Reason 1: The Nails Weren't Prepped Properly

This is the number one cause of early lifting — and it's 100% in the hands of the nail technician.

Before any gel touches your nail, the plate needs to be completely free of oil, moisture and residue. Any trace of natural oil on the nail prevents the gel from bonding to the surface — no matter how good the product is.

Professional prep involves pushing back and gently removing the cuticle from the nail plate, lightly buffing the surface to create texture for the gel to grip, dehydrating the nail with a prep solution, and applying a primer or bonder before the base coat.

Skip any of those steps and the gel will lift, no matter what.

At Mammie's, every gel set starts with a full nail prep routine. It adds a few minutes to the appointment — but it's the difference between nails that last a week and nails that last three.

Reason 2: The Gel Was Applied Too Thick

Thicker is not better when it comes to gel. Each layer needs to cure completely under the UV/LED lamp before the next one goes on — and thick layers don't cure all the way through.

When the bottom of a layer stays soft because it didn't cure properly, the whole layer is structurally weak and will begin to separate from the nail. The result looks like lifting but the actual problem is an uncured base.

The fix is thin, even layers — typically three to four coats maximum — cured fully between each one.

Reason 3: You Have Naturally Oily Nail Beds

Some people simply produce more natural oils on their nails than others. This isn't a problem — it just means the prep step needs to be more thorough. If you've been told before that gel "just doesn't work" on your nails, it was almost certainly a preparation issue, not a body chemistry issue.

Tell your nail artist upfront if you've had lifting problems in the past. At Mammie's, we'll adjust the prep routine — using a stronger dehydrator and a bonding primer — to compensate for higher natural oil production.

Reason 4: The Edges Weren't Capped

Take a look at where your lifting always starts. It's almost always at the free edge — the tip of the nail. That's because the tip is the most exposed part of the nail and experiences the most stress from daily activities.

The solution is simple: capping the edge. This means running a thin stripe of gel along the very tip of the nail at every layer — base coat, colour coat and top coat. It seals the edge and prevents moisture and impact from getting underneath the product.

It's a small technique detail that adds significant wear time. Ask your technician specifically whether they cap edges — if they don't know what you mean, that's a red flag.

Reason 5: Exposure to Water Too Soon After Your Appointment

Even when gel is cured under a lamp, it continues to harden slightly over the following 24 hours as the product fully sets. Extended water exposure during this window — long baths, washing hair, doing dishes — introduces moisture under the product before it has fully bonded.

For the first 24 hours after your gel appointment:

  • Avoid long soaks in the bath or hot tub
  • Wear gloves if you're doing the dishes
  • Try to wash your hair before your appointment rather than after
  • Avoid steam rooms and saunas

After 24 hours, your gel is fully set and normal daily contact with water is completely fine.

Bonus: The Daily Habit That Makes the Biggest Difference

Cuticle oil. This is the single best thing you can do between appointments to extend your wear and keep your nails healthy. Applying cuticle oil once or twice a day keeps the skin around the nail flexible and moisturised — which reduces the stress that causes chips and lifting at the sides.

It also makes your nails look freshly done for longer. A small bottle of cuticle oil is one of the best investments you can make in your nail care routine.

"Our Gel Nails start at R350 and our Acrylics at R450 — both done with professional-grade products and a full prep routine. If your nails from somewhere else aren't lasting, come in and let us show you what properly done nails feel like. There's a noticeable difference."
— Lerato, Nail Artist, Mammie's Beauty Palace

A Quick Summary

  • Prep thoroughly — dehydrate, prime, remove oil from the nail plate before any product
  • Apply thin layers — cure fully between each coat
  • Mention oily nails — so your technician adjusts the routine
  • Cap the edges — every layer, every time
  • Stay dry for 24 hours — give the gel time to fully set
  • Use cuticle oil daily — it's the easiest habit that makes the biggest difference
